### Post by cabber on 2006-03-29
I have been playing around with Fedora and want to dual boot with Ubuntu.  I'm not sure how the chain loader thing works for setting up the grub so I will post what I have and could use some advice.  I'm using the standard kernel for Ubuntu64(off the DVD disk).  Here is my current grub:

```
# grub.conf generated by anaconda
#
# Note that you do not have to rerun grub after making changes to this file
# NOTICE:  You have a /boot partition.  This means that
#          all kernel and initrd paths are relative to /boot/, eg.
#          root (hd0,0)
#          kernel /vmlinuz-version ro root=/dev/VolGroup00/LogVol00
#          initrd /initrd-version.img
#boot=/dev/sda
default=0
timeout=10
splashimage=(hd0,0)/grub/splash.xpm.gz
hiddenmenu
title Fedora Core (2.6.16-1.2080_FC5)
	root (hd0,0)
	kernel /vmlinuz-2.6.16-1.2080_FC5 ro root=/dev/VolGroup00/LogVol00 rhgb quiet
	initrd /initrd-2.6.16-1.2080_FC5.img
title Fedora Core (2.6.15-1.2054_FC5)
	root (hd0,0)
	kernel /vmlinuz-2.6.15-1.2054_FC5 ro root=/dev/VolGroup00/LogVol00 rhgb quiet
	initrd /initrd-2.6.15-1.2054_FC5.img
```

Here is my fdisk -1

Ubuntu is currently installed on **/dev/sdc**

### Post by Sutekh on 2006-04-01
Chainloading is unneccesary for Linux, only Windows/DOS IIRC.

What are the contents of the device map?  This file; /boot/grub/device.map contains the information that GRUB uses to 'map' device locations, such as sda1, to a format that GRUB likes, such as (hd0,0)

If you check out the file
```
cat /boot/grub/device.map
```
You should get something similar to this  (I'm just guessing mind you)
```
(hd0) /dev/sda
(hd1) /dev/sdb
**(hd2) /dev/sdc**
```
So in this example /dev/sdc is mapped to hd2.

So the entry you would need to boot Ubuntu from sdc1 would be something like this
```
title Ubuntu, kernel 2.6.12-9-386
**root (hd2,0)**
kernel /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.12-9-386 **root=/dev/sdc1** ro quiet splash
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.12-9-386
savedefault
boot

```
 - the **title** is just a description of the GRUB entry

 - the **root** is the location of the root filesystem (sdc1 -> hd2,0)

 - the **kernel** refers to the kernel image.  It is found in /boot and you must make sure you get the filename correct.  The **root=/dev/sdc1** is also important to get right

 - the **initrd** refers to the initial ramdisc.  Again found in /boot, make sure the filename is correct for the kernel you want to boot

So check out the device.map and see if you can't fix it up.

### Post by Sutekh on 2006-04-03
[QUOTE=cabber]Sutekh,

Thanks so much for the detailed response.  I am beginning to play around with different distros on a dedicated Linux machine (no windows install).  You mentioned with Linux that chainloading is not necessary.  What if the distro I install uses LILO for the booting process.  For instance, I have Ubuntu installed with Grub and want to install PCLinuxOS.  I'm not sure how to configure grub to boot to multiple distros.[/QUOTE]
So I understand what you are asking, you want to install Ubuntu and PCLinuxOS, but use GRUB as the bootloader?

Have you installed PCLinuxOS before? (I haven't)  

Are you given the option of where you install Lilo?

If you can, install Lilo on the same partition as PCLinuxOS.  So if PCLinuxOS is on /dev/hda2, then install Lilo on /dev/hda2 NOT /dev/hda as this puts it on the MBR.  I have found that when installing multiple distributions it is easier if later bootloaders are installed on the root partition of the distro rather than the MBR.

Boot up Ubuntu and view Lilo's menu configuration file (I assume it has one, again no experience with Lilo) and copy the relevant details down, such as the root filesystem location, kernel image location/name, initial ramdisc (initrd) location/name, etc.  All the stuff that I posted before, for a stock GRUB entry.  **That basic information is also given as example in the second link at the bottom of this post**

Then you can edit GRUB's menu.lst and add this information for PCLinuxOS.  It shouldn't be too hard, as Linux distributions all seem to me that they boot in the same way.


In case you want to do the opposite, install Lilo as the main bootloader instead of GRUB, you should read these links.


[PCLinuxOS Wiki - Add Linux to Lilo](http://www.pclinuxonline.com/wiki/AddLinuxToLilo)

- This next link has an example of a typical Lilo boot entry, from its configuration file /etc/lilo.conf

[PCLinuxOS Wiki - Add PCLinuxOS to existing Lilo](http://www.pclinuxonline.com/wiki/AddPCLosToLilo)

[The Linux Doc Project - Lilo Mini-HOWTO](http://tldp.org/HOWTO/LILO.html)

---
